---
title: "Cooperative & Legacy Genres"
aliases: ["Co-op Games", "Legacy Games", "Campaign Boardgames", "Shared-Win Games"]
family: "Systems & Mechanics"
media: [tabletop, live-game]
facets:
  effects: [belonging, tension]
  problems: [content-cost, domination, pacing-failure]
  components: [locks-and-concealment, paper-artefacts, physical-parts, state-and-resource-trackers]
  genre: [fantasy, historical, horror, sci-fi]
provenance: "researched"
---

*Silhouette: Two boardgame genres built on a single shared fate, one across a table and one across a campaign that never resets.*

A cooperative boardgame turns the table around. Instead of players ranged against each other, everyone faces the same threat and shares one win and one loss. The opponent is the system itself, a set of rules that spreads disease or lands settlers on its own, and the whole group either holds it back or goes under together. A legacy game adds a second axis. It refuses to reset. Stickers go on the board and stay. Cards get torn up and are gone. Sealed boxes open once and cannot be sealed again. The game keeps a memory, and that memory shapes every session after.

The two genres are distinct, and each works on its own, but they fit together so naturally that their most celebrated titles are hybrids. A cooperative group is already bound to a shared outcome, so adding permanent consequence raises the stakes of that outcome rather than splitting the table. This article is about both engines. It covers what each one does to the feel of play and which failure modes each invites, then turns to the reason they are so often welded into one box.

## The Mechanism (How it works)

### The cooperative inversion

In a cooperative boardgame all players share a single win condition and a single loss condition, and the opposition is an automated set of rules rather than any person at the table. [researched] That one structural change reshapes nearly every other decision a designer makes. It governs how roles are assigned and what information stays hidden, the way the threat escalates, and how difficulty is tuned. The format became a commercially distinct genre between roughly 2000 and 2010. Reiner Knizia's *The Lord of the Rings* (2000) showed that a large publisher could sell a cooperative title, and Matt Leacock's *Pandemic* (2008) was the breakout that carried the format into the mainstream. [researched]

*Pandemic* remains the clearest model of how the genre works. Players take specialist roles and move across a network of cities, removing disease cubes while racing to research four cures before any of several loss conditions trips. The engine is the epidemic card shuffled into the player deck. Each epidemic raises the infection rate and tips the discard pile back onto the infection deck, so already-stricken cities flare again at an accelerating pace. [researched] The threat is not a hostile player making clever moves; it is a feedback loop the rules drive on their own. Difficulty is a dial built into that loop, set by inserting four to six epidemic cards before the game begins. [researched]

The way the system stands in for an opponent connects the genre to questions the rest of this family treats elsewhere. How a single threat reads as fair or arbitrary is the matter of [Encounter & Challenge Design](encounter-and-challenge-design.qmd), and the broader question of whether one player or a substitute can stand in for the whole opposition is [Solo Modes](solo-modes.qmd). A cooperative game that supports several roles is, almost for free, a solo game, since one person can run two or more roles at once. *Pandemic*, *Spirit Island*, *Aeon's End*, and *Mage Knight* all play solo as readily as in a group. [researched]

### The quarterbacking problem

The cooperative format carries one signature failure mode, and it is structural rather than a matter of bad manners. When all information is open, an experienced or assertive player works out the best move for every role and begins dictating plays. Cooperative play then collapses into one person playing alone with an audience. [researched] The boardgame world calls this quarterbacking; the live-game tradition calls the same thing the alpha player. [researched] Leacock has said plainly that the problem is intrinsic to open-information cooperative games and can only be mitigated, never removed. [researched] The designer Erik Twice reframes it usefully as a symptom with three roots: too little game depth, so one mind can compute the whole table's best play; a skill gap between players; and plain behaviour. [researched]

Every mitigation is a way of making total optimisation by one person impossible. The strongest is structural hidden information. *Spirit Island* (R. Eric Reuss, 2017) gives each player a distinct spirit with its own powers and a hidden hand, so no single player has the bandwidth to model every role at once, and *Aeon's End* hides hands the same way. [researched] *The Crew* (Thomas Sing, 2019) restricts what may be said: a single signal token per round means nobody can talk another player through their hand. [researched] Randomised turn order does similar work. *Aeon's End* draws turn order from a separate breach deck, so even the next player to act is uncertain and the planning horizon stays short. [researched] Deepening specialist roles across a campaign helps too, because it makes each player the acknowledged authority on their own role's options. [researched] The live forms of these fixes are the same idea in another room, treated player-side in [Cooperative vs Adversarial Formats](../players-social-dynamics/cooperative-vs-adversarial-formats.qmd): physical escape rooms resist the alpha player largely because they run on a clock, and time pressure forecloses the long directive monologue. [researched]

There is a counter-intuitive finding worth carrying into any cooperative design. A study of escape-room teams found that the more successful groups communicated more tensely, not less. [researched] Productive cooperation runs on friction, disagreement, and the renegotiation of plans. Designing for frictionless consensus invites the alpha-player collapse, because friction is what keeps several minds genuinely in the game.

### Two ways the outcome can feel unearned

A second pair of failure modes attacks the satisfaction of the result itself, and they are mirror images. One is the "we won by luck" complaint, where a run of good fortune hands the group an unearned win. The other is "we lost arbitrarily", where a bad run sinks the group through no fault of how it played. [researched] A cooperative game that leans hard on random draws faces both at once. The fix is legibility. *Aeon's End* sequences its deck deterministically, by turning the spent discard pile over without a shuffle, so card order is preserved and managing it becomes a planning problem several turns deep. [researched] *Spirit Island* makes its Invaders act from fixed, readable action tables, so a bad turn reads as a consequence of choices rather than a cruel shuffle. [researched]

The same care separates a good threat engine from a bad one. *Pandemic*'s epidemic mechanic is a self-reinforcing spiral by design, where each bad outcome worsens the board and makes the next outcome likelier to be bad, but that escalation is the intended narrative engine. [researched] A designer has to separate intended escalation, which is the drama, from arbitrary cascades that feel unfair and produce the "nothing we could have done" loss. [synthesis] How much of an outcome should ride on the randomiser at all, and how much should emerge from legible decisions, is the wider craft of [Elegance, Emergence & Randomness](elegance-emergence-and-randomness.qmd).

### The legacy refusal to reset

A legacy boardgame is built for a finite campaign in which player actions produce permanent, irreversible changes to the game state and its physical components, and to the rules themselves. [researched] Stickers stay on the board. Torn cards are gone. Sealed packets, once opened, cannot be closed. The defining property is irreversibility. The game accumulates a history, and that history shapes every later session. This differs from a long campaign, which keeps persistent characters but stable rules, and from optional expansion content a group can take or leave. In a legacy game the campaign is the design. A complete arc is baked into the structure, and the game knows where it sits along that arc. [researched]

Rob Daviau, who with Chris Dupuis published the first legacy title in *Risk Legacy* (2011), described the genesis as a question about a basic assumption of the hobby: why do games reset between sessions as though no history happened? [researched] *Risk Legacy* runs fifteen games. Players write on the board in permanent marker, sticker territories, and tear up cards that then leave the game for good. Faction stickers placed in game one are still there in game fifteen. [researched] Legacy design rejects the reset convention outright, and decisions matter precisely because they cannot be undone.

A small set of techniques carries the genre. The most theatrical is the sealed-packet reveal: rules, components, and characters are withheld inside sealed packets, and the campaign structure controls when each opens. Tearing open a new packet mid-session is the functional equivalent of a chapter-ending cliff-hanger. [researched] Its characteristic risk is that a mechanic delivered this way can feel arbitrary if the campaign has not prepared players for it, the "punitive reveal," which good design heads off with foreshadowing and rules that grow out of prior sessions. [researched] The second technique is the branching campaign. *Pandemic Legacy: Season 1* has explicit branches where the outcome of certain months decides which path the campaign follows, and *Gloomhaven*'s scenario unlocks turn on choices made in play, so no two groups end up with the same campaign. [researched] That branch-and-unlock structure is a near-cousin of the shapes mapped in [Branching Narrative Craft](../structure-pacing/branching-narrative-craft.qmd) and sits inside the longer-form question of [Campaign Arc Shapes](../structure-pacing/campaign-arc-shapes.qmd).

Permanence is also a materials problem, not only a rules one. A box that is consumed by playing it depends on components designed to be marked, torn, and sealed, which puts the genre in direct contact with the prop-side craft of [Materials, Durability & Reset](../space-props-materiality/materials-durability-and-reset.qmd): legacy is the rare format that wants the opposite of resetability, and engineers irreversibility on purpose.

::: {.callout-note}
### The Specimen
*Pandemic Legacy: Season 1* (Matt Leacock and Rob Daviau, 2015) is the title that proved both genres at once. It grafts the legacy format onto an already-canonical cooperative game: a twelve-month arc played across twelve to twenty-four sessions, each permanently altering the state through rule changes, stickers, card destruction, and sealed-packet reveals. Disease mechanics evolve and stay evolved; the world map collects stickers recording outbreaks, quarantines, and permanent damage; a failed month is marked for good. [researched] The Spiel des Jahres jury, awarding it the Kennerspiel des Jahres 2016, singled out the irreversibility of decisions and the campaign's narrative cohesion, the feeling of being a protagonist in a serial drama. [researched] Role specialisation also deepens across the campaign, which doubles as one structural brake on quarterbacking. The cooperative group was already invested in a shared outcome; the legacy layer made that investment last twelve sessions, and that is the whole argument for combining them. [synthesis]
:::

### Why the two genres fuse

The legacy format pairs unusually well with cooperative play, and the reason is straightforward. A cooperative group already shares an outcome, so the longitudinal stakes that legacy adds, permanent losses, permanent unlocks, characters who retire, land on a table that is already pulling in one direction. [researched] The team remembers what happened in session three, and the board remembers too. *Pandemic Legacy*, *Aeon's End Legacy* (2019), *Sleeping Gods* (2021), and *Tainted Grail* (2019) all run on this combination, and *Gloomhaven* (Isaac Childres, 2017) carries it across ninety-five or more scenarios with permanent character advancement and sealed envelopes that unlock new classes and mechanics. [researched] The fusion is not the only path. *Spirit Island* sustains deep cooperative play with no legacy mechanics at all, leaning on asymmetry and scenario variety, while *Pandemic Legacy* uses the legacy structure to carry a cooperative experience that would otherwise exhaust its novelty far sooner. [researched]

Legacy is best understood as a deliberate inversion of the replayability problem, and seeing it that way clarifies the design choice. A replayable game sustains play across unlimited sessions by varying the experience each time, so history is irrelevant and every session resets. A legacy game instead sustains a bounded run of twelve to twenty-four sessions by making each session the only time it will ever happen. The accumulated history is the whole point of playing. [researched] Both approaches set out to keep a group engaged over a long run of play. Neither beats the other. The trade is laid out in full in [Replayability](replayability.qmd), which is the genre's structural opposite.

That trade also names legacy's hardest cost. The single-play problem is that a legacy campaign is largely unplayable once finished, because the box has been consumed by playing it. [researched] Designers have answered in three ways: treat single use as a feature and price it as a one-off experience, as *Pandemic Legacy* does; or sell a reset path, the separately bought Recharge Packs of *Charterstone* (Jamey Stegmaier, 2017) and the reset cards included in the box of *My City* (Reiner Knizia, 2020), which let a fresh group play the whole campaign again. [researched] A longer-form failure to watch is the boring middle, where a campaign sags in its central episodes after the novelty of permanent change has worn off but before the climax is in sight, which tight pacing and sub-arcs within the larger arc are there to fix. [researched]

### The same shape in a live room

Both engines reappear, almost unchanged, in live play. The cooperative format is the native one for escape rooms, which are the dominant cooperative live medium, and the alpha-player problem there is structurally the same as quarterbacking at a table: one dominant solver drives everyone else, and the standard defence is the same clock and the same distribution of materials so that every participant is actually reading and investigating. [researched] The cooperative-deduction strand has a clean live ancestor in *Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective* (1981 to 1982), which runs with no game master and no fixed clue order, the team working a casebook in whatever sequence it likes, and which as the first cooperative game to win the Spiel des Jahres (1985) is a historical anchor for the open-investigation format that later campaign and legacy mysteries inherit. [researched]

The legacy idea has crossed over too, and the bridge is a physical one. The *Exit: The Game* series of cooperative escape-room boxes is played by writing on, folding, tearing, and cutting its components, so each box gives a single play and is consumed exactly the way a legacy boardgame is. [researched] The same permanence that makes a legacy campaign unrepeatable makes an *Exit* box a one-and-done evening, which is the legacy genre's single-play problem reappearing as a feature in a live-flavoured product. The shared-fate co-op format also has a direct player-side counterpart in [Cooperative vs Adversarial Formats](../players-social-dynamics/cooperative-vs-adversarial-formats.qmd), where the same five-position spectrum from fully cooperative to hidden traitor is read for its social effect rather than its mechanics. That hidden-traitor end, where a cooperative frame is disrupted by a concealed defector, is its own design space treated in [Social Performance Genres](social-performance-genres.qmd); titles like *Shadows over Camelot* (2005) and *Battlestar Galactica* (2008) sit on the seam between the cooperative genre and social deduction. [researched]

## Where this fits

**The bigger picture.** These two genres are less a pair of mechanics than a pair of stances on what a game is for. The cooperative stance moves the contest from between the players to between the table and the system, so much of its craft is really about protecting the group from itself: quarterbacking, luck legibility, and the runaway spiral are all problems of a shared mind rather than a clever opponent. The legacy stance moves the stakes from inside a session to across a campaign, trading replayability for the weight of permanence. Each can stand alone, yet each draws on the wider Systems family to do its work. It needs the threat engine of [Encounter & Challenge Design](encounter-and-challenge-design.qmd) and the randomness budget of [Elegance, Emergence & Randomness](elegance-emergence-and-randomness.qmd). They fuse so readily because a shared outcome and a permanent one amplify rather than fight each other, and the genre's most acclaimed titles live in that overlap.

**Next steps.** To weigh the legacy trade directly, go to its structural opposite, [Replayability](replayability.qmd), which sustains play by resetting rather than remembering. The single-player reduction of the cooperative format, where one person runs the table or a bot stands in for the group, is [Solo Modes](solo-modes.qmd). When the cooperative frame is deliberately broken by a hidden defector, the craft turns to [Social Performance Genres](social-performance-genres.qmd) and, player-side, to [Cooperative vs Adversarial Formats](../players-social-dynamics/cooperative-vs-adversarial-formats.qmd). For the choices that make a shared turn worth taking and a permanent decision feel heavy, see [Decision Space & Choice Design](decision-space-and-choice-design.qmd), and for the economy of who acts when, see [Turn Structure & Action Economy](turn-structure-and-action-economy.qmd). And because a campaign is a bounded arc, the longer-form structure of one is [Campaign Arc Shapes](../structure-pacing/campaign-arc-shapes.qmd).

## Sources

- *The Lord of the Rings* (Reiner Knizia, Kosmos / Wizards of the Coast, 2000); the first widely visible cooperative title. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_of_the_Rings_(board_game)>
- *Pandemic* (Matt Leacock, Z-Man Games, 2008); the epidemic feedback loop and epidemic-card difficulty dial. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pandemic_(board_game)> and <https://www.leacock.com/pandemic>
- *Spirit Island* (R. Eric Reuss, Greater Than Games, 2017); asymmetric spirits, hidden hands, readable Invader action tables, adversary-tier difficulty. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spirit_Island_(board_game)> and <https://rericreuss.com/game/spirit-island/>
- *Aeon's End* (Kevin Riley et al., Indie Boards and Cards, 2016); the non-shuffled deck and breach-deck turn order. <https://indieboardsandcards.com/our-games/aeons-end/>
- *The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine* (Thomas Sing, KOSMOS, 2019); cooperative trick-taking under a one-token communication restriction. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Crew_(card_game)>
- *Mage Knight Board Game* (Vlaada Chvátil, WizKids, 2011); heavy co-op with strong solo overlap. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mage_Knight_Board_Game>
- Erik Twice, "The alpha player in co-ops is a symptom, not the disease" (2021); the three structural roots of quarterbacking. <https://eriktwice.com>
- Almaatouq et al., "The anatomy of social dynamics in escape rooms," *Scientific Reports* (2022); successful cooperative teams communicate more tensely, not less. <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-23900-8>
- *Risk Legacy* (Rob Daviau and Chris Dupuis, Avalon Hill / Hasbro, 2011); the first legacy boardgame, fifteen-game campaign, permanent marker and torn cards. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Risk_Legacy>
- Rob Daviau, interview on the legacy system (GameSkinny); the genesis of legacy design as a rejection of the reset convention. <https://www.gameskinny.com/culture/an-interview-with-rob-daviau-discussing-the-legacy-system-board-games-and-more/>
- *Pandemic Legacy: Season 1* (Matt Leacock and Rob Daviau, Z-Man Games, 2015); the co-op + legacy hybrid; Kennerspiel des Jahres 2016. <https://www.spiel-des-jahres.de/en/games/pandemic-legacy-season-1/> and <https://www.leacock.com/pandemic-legacy>
- *Gloomhaven* (Isaac Childres, Cephalofair Games, 2017); campaign dungeon crawl, sealed envelopes, permanent character state. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gloomhaven>
- *Charterstone* (Jamey Stegmaier, Stonemaier Games, 2017); the persistent village and separately sold Recharge Packs as a reset path. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charterstone>
- *My City* (Reiner Knizia, KOSMOS, 2020); twenty-four-game campaign with in-box reset cards. <https://www.knizia.de/my-city-our-legacy-game/>
- *Shadows over Camelot* (Serge Laget and Bruno Cathala, Days of Wonder, 2005) and *Battlestar Galactica: The Board Game* (Corey Konieczka, Fantasy Flight Games, 2008); the co-op / hidden-traitor hybrid. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shadows_over_Camelot>
- *Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective* (Raymond Edwards, Suzanne Goldberg and Gary Grady, Sleuth Publications, 1981 to 1982; Space Cowboys edition 2017); no-GM open-investigation co-op, first cooperative game to win the Spiel des Jahres (1985). <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes_Consulting_Detective>
- *Exit: The Game* (Inka and Markus Brand, KOSMOS, 2016 onward); cooperative escape-room boxes consumed by writing on, folding, and tearing components. <https://www.boardgamequest.com/exit-the-game-review/>

<!-- BACKLINKS:START (generated by scripts/backlinks.py — do not edit by hand) -->

## Referenced by

- [Cooperative vs Adversarial Formats](../players-social-dynamics/cooperative-vs-adversarial-formats.qmd)
- [Euro vs Ameritrash: The Two Design Poles](euro-vs-ameritrash-the-two-design-poles.qmd)
- [Fabrication & Sourcing](../space-props-materiality/fabrication-and-sourcing.qmd)
- [Materials, Durability & Reset](../space-props-materiality/materials-durability-and-reset.qmd)
- [Pandemic](../../exemplars/pandemic.qmd)
- [Replayability](replayability.qmd)
- [Social Performance Genres](social-performance-genres.qmd)
- [Solo Modes](solo-modes.qmd)

<!-- BACKLINKS:END -->
